coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient ferric ion sensing and high resolution latent fingerprint imaging based on fluorescent silicon quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Di
Zhao, Zihe
coredata.dc:description: Expanding the application of silicon based luminescent materials is a fast-growing interdisciplinary field. Herein, a novel fluorescent bifunctional probe based on silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) for highly sensitive Fe3+ sensing and high-resolution latent fingerprint (LFP) imaging was subtly devised. The SiQD solution was mildly prepared using 3-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane as the silicon source and sodium ascorbate as the reductant, showing green emission at 515 nm under UV irradiation with a quantum yield of 19.8%. As a highly sensitive fluorescent sensor, the SiQD was demonstrated to have a highly selective quenching with Fe3+ in the concentration range of 2–1000 μM with the LOD of 0.086 μM in water. The quenching rate constant and association constant of the SiQDs-Fe3+ complex was calculated to be 1.05 × 1012 mol/s and 6.8 × 103 L/mol, respectively, suggesting a static quenching effect between them. Moreover, to achieve high-resolution LFP imaging, a novel SiO2@SiQDs composite powder was further fabricated. The SiQDs were covalently anchored on the surface of silica nanospheres to conquer aggregation-caused quenching for the high-solid fluorescence. In the demonstration of LFP imaging, this silicon based luminescent composite exhibited high developing sensitivity, high selectivity and high contrast, indicating its practical value as a fingerprint developer at crime scenes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon quantum dot
Fluorescent probe
Fe3+ sensing
Static quenching
Latent fingerprint imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity measurement of Fe3+ sensing
Metal detection in real water sample
Application to the real water samples

Southwest University of Political Science and Law

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by Chongqing Municipal Bureau of Science and Technology (cstc2021jcyj-msxmX0713, CSTB2022TIAD-KPX0109), Chongqing Education Commission (KJZD-M202000301, KJZD-K202200302), Innovation Research Group Project of Universities in Chongqing (CXQT21033), and Southwest University of Political Science and Law (2021XZNDJDYB-07, 2021XZXS-137).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of SiQDs. (b) Absorption and fluorescence spectra of the SiQDs. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of SiQDs obtained under different excitations. (d) PL lifetime decays curves of SiQDs.

(a), (b) Fluorescence spectra (3D) of the SiQDs with different concentration of Fe3+. (c) Changing of color with different concentrations of Fe3+. (d) Relationship between F/F0 and concentration of Fe3+.
(a) Absorption and fluorescence spectra SiQDs and SiQDs-Fe3+. (b) PL lifetime decays curves of SiQDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+. (c) Probable mechanism of the bonding between SiQDs and Fe3+
SEM and TEM image of SiO2 and SiO2@SiQDs.

(a) Absorption and fluorescence spectra of the SiO2@SiQDs. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of SiO2@SiQDs obtained under different excitations. (c) PL lifetime decays curves of SiQDs. (d) FT-IR spectra of SiO2@SiQDs and SiO2.
Image of fresh LFPs developed by SiO2@SiQDs powders on various surfaces in the bright field (a-f) and then observed under 365 nm UV light (a’-f’).
Images of 15-d LFP developed by different powders (a, b) and related grey analyses between the fingerprint ridges (a’, b’).
Schematic illustration of synthesis of SiQDs and for Fe3+ sensing, LFP detection.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient ferric ion sensing and high resolution latent fingerprint imaging based on fluorescent silicon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

Southwest University of Political Science and Law

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Intelligent Justice (Southwest University of Political Science and Law), Ministry of Education, Chongqing 401102, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Intelligent Justice (Southwest University of Political Science and Law)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Chongqing Institutes of Higher Education Key Forensic Science Laboratory, Criminal Investigation School, Southwest University of Political Science and Law, Chongqing 401102, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Institutes of Higher Education Key Forensic Science Laboratory
Southwest University of Political Science and Law

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Expanding the application of silicon based luminescent materials is a fast-growing interdisciplinary field. Herein, a novel fluorescent bifunctional probe based on silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) for highly sensitive Fe3+ sensing and high-resolution latent fingerprint (LFP) imaging was subtly devised. The SiQD solution was mildly prepared using 3-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane as the silicon source and sodium ascorbate as the reductant, showing green emission at 515 nm under UV irradiation with a quantum yield of 19.8%. As a highly sensitive fluorescent sensor, the SiQD was demonstrated to have a highly selective quenching with Fe3+ in the concentration range of 2–1000 μM with the LOD of 0.086 μM in water. The quenching rate constant and association constant of the SiQDs-Fe3+ complex was calculated to be 1.05 × 1012 mol/s and 6.8 × 103 L/mol, respectively, suggesting a static quenching effect between them. Moreover, to achieve high-resolution LFP imaging, a novel SiO2@SiQDs composite powder was further fabricated. The SiQDs were covalently anchored on the surface of silica nanospheres to conquer aggregation-caused quenching for the high-solid fluorescence. In the demonstration of LFP imaging, this silicon based luminescent composite exhibited high developing sensitivity, high selectivity and high contrast, indicating its practical value as a fingerprint developer at crime scenes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most essential trace elements in the human body, Fe3+ plays an indispensable role in cell metabolism, protein synthesis, enzyme catalysis and other life processes [1]. However, excessive intake of Fe3+ is proved to cause anemia, liver injury, arthritis and other diseases [2]. Consequently, both EU and USEPA have approved a permissible limit of 0.3 mg/L in drinking water for Fe3+. Furthermore, in view of the fact that the excessive iron content can impact the sensory properties of products, there are strict regulations on its level in industrial water in papermaking, food, textile, plastics and other manufacturing. Therefore, to realize the qualitative and quantitative detection of ferric ions in water with high sensitivity remains hot topics for human health, environmental protection and industrial production. So far, various approaches have been developed for Fe3+ detection, including atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS)[3], voltammetry [4], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS)[5], electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR)[6], colorimetric analysis [7], et al. Unfortunately, the complicated operation of instruments and time-consuming procedures of sample pretreatment greatly restrict their further application in ferric ion sensing. Compared to traditional approaches and recently reported colorimetric assay, fluorescence sensing based on quantum dots (QDs) has drawn much attention in ion sensing field due to its high sensitivity, high selectivity, low detection limit and ease of operation. Considering the toxicity of heavy metal-containing QDs early reported, seeking nontoxic QDs as fluorescent probes thereby becomes increasingly concerned in fluorescence sensing.

Recent years have witnessed the fast growth in metal ion sensing by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as fluorescent probes [8,9]. As another class of nonpoisonous nanoparticles with richness in resources and excellent optical performance, silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) have increasingly shown potentials in bio-imaging [10,11], photoelectric devices [12], medical diagnosis [13], drug delivery [14]. However, their utilization in Fe3+ sensing was seldom reported so far. In 2020, Adinarayana and coworkers [15] firstly announced a successful synthesis of highly stable aqueous soluble silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) for the detection of Fe(III) with the LOD of 12.4 ppb using the silicon-rich extract of “Equisetum arvense” as a biological precursor. Afterward, wheat straw-based SiQDs (WS-SiQDs)[16] and other water-soluble SiNPs [17] were also applied as fluorescent probes for iron ion detection. Although the above SiQDs-based methods have demonstrated decent performance in Fe3+ sensing, it is of great scientific and practical significance for developing more convenient and milder synthesis of SiQDs in ways of more sensitivity, wider detection range and deeper understanding of fluorescence quenching mechanism.

In addition, the employment of fluorescent nanomaterials, such as carbonized nanoparticles [18–20], lanthanide nanoparticles [21–23], manganese-doped red-emitting phosphors [24], organic aggregate luminogens [25,26], and perovskite nanocrystals [27] in latent fingerprint (LFP) imaging have been paid extensive concentration due to their high sensitivity, high selectivity, and high contrast in recent decades. In forensic science, the powder dusting is still regarded as the most efficient measure at crime scenes because of its promptness, portability and easy accessibility for operators [28]. Regrettably, as a result of the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect [29,30], SiQD powders often suffer a decline in fluorescence compared to their colloidal state, which would lead to a lower developing contrast for LFPs. Hence, the successful application of SiQDs in LFP imaging depends largely on how to maintain the SSF and to improve the particle flowability of SiQD powders. Considering that current SiQD powders for LFP imaging were almost prepared by natural drying from their colloidal solution [31–33], there is an urgent requirement to design a novel monodisperse SiQD powder to conquer ACQ effect for forensic investigation.

Here, we have developed a bifunctional probe based on fluorescent SiQDs, one is colloidal solution for highly sensitive sensing of Fe3+, another is SiO2@SiQDs composite powders for LFP imaging (Scheme 1 ). On the one side, our SiQD solution proved that its fluorescence could be linearly attenuated by Fe3+ in water through the static quenching in the range of 2–1000 μM and the LOD was determined to be 0.086 μM. This novel Fe3+ fluorescent probe was prepared at room temperature and atmospheric conditions and evidenced as a highly sensitive and highly selective sensing technique. On the other side, we have engineered an amination reaction, during which the monodisperse silica nanospheres and as-prepared SiQDs were chemically bonded, so as to immobilize fluorescent QDs on silica surface without causing ACQ by agglomeration. With the help of squirrel brush and UV excitation, our SiO2@SiQD powder can produce high-resolution fingerprint images, which poses a promising fluorescent probe for forensic investigation.

All chemical reagents were of analytic grade and used with no purification. Methanol, ethanol, FeCl3, CaCl2, CrCl3·6H20, CoCl2·6H20, ZnCl2, CuCl2·2H20, MnCl2, NiCl2·6H2O, AlCl3, Hg(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2, and NaNO2 were all supplied from Chuandong Chemical group (Chongqing, China). Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), 3-Aminopropyl trimethoxysilane (APTMS) and Sodium ascorbate (SA) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin BiochemicalTechnologyCo., Ltd. The commercial green phosphor for fingerprint development was provided by Chongqing Dunyin Technology Co., Ltd (China).

Fluorescence excitation, emission spectra, quantum yield, and decay curves of all liquid samples were recorded on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-6000, SHIMADZU, Japan), while those of the powder samples were measured by Fluoromax-4C-L TCSPC (HORIBA, Japan). The morphology of silica nanosphere and SiO2@SiQD powder were tested by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan) and a scanning electron microscope (SEM, S3800, Hitachi, Japan). UV–vis spectra of liquid and powder were both obtained using SPECORD 200 PLUS spectrophotometer (Analytik Jena AG, Germany). Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet iS10, Thermo Scientific, USA) was used to analyze the surface chemical bonds of all samples. The surface composition and elemental analyses was performed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Nexsa G2, Thermo Scientific, USA). The measurement of metal elements in real water sample was applied using ICP-MS 7700 (Agilent, USA).

SiQDs were facilely synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. Under the room temperature and atmospheric pressure, 1.6 mL of ATPMS and 2 mL SA (100 mM) were successively mixed and then added into a glass beaker containing 6.4 mL ultrapure water. The total synthesis was completed in 1 h and with no stirring, during which the colorless solution gradually become pale yellow. The as-prepared yellowish solution was purified with a 500 Da cutoff dialysis bag against ultrapure water for 24 h. The final SiQD solution was kept in a dark field after centrifugation and membrane filtration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity measurement of Fe3+ sensing
ce:section.ce:para: For selective determination of Fe3+, stock solutions of various metal ions (Fe3+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Na+, Ca2+, Al3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cr3+) were firstly prepared by ultrapure water. Different metal ion solution samples (100 μM) were added to the previous SiQD solution. Secondly, the fluorescence intensity of these mixtures at 515 nm under 380 nm excitation was separately measured. The sensitive determination of Fe3+ was performed in an aqueous solution at room temperature. Simply, Fe3+ solutions of different concentrations (0–1000 µM) were prepared in advance, and then 5 μL of each sample was orderly added into SiQD solution for the fluorescence intensity analysis in the same way.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal detection in real water sample
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the practicability of SiQD probe, real water samples were also analyzed. They were collected from Yuxiu Lake on our campus, which was first filtered through a 0.08 mm membrane and measured by ICP-MS to check the actual components of various metal ions. A series of samples were prepared through step-by-step dilution of the standard solution, and the detection was implemented according to the preceding procedures.

The silica nanospheres were prepared by a modified Stöber method. Typically, 100 mL methanol, 5 mL deionized water and 8 mL ammonia (25–28 wt%) were mixed under magnetic stirring at 4000 rpm for 10 min at room temperature. Then, 5 mL TEOS was then added to the mixture drop by drop. The above mixed solution was under magnetic stirred for 2.5 h at 35 ℃ and then collected by high-speed centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 10 min and washed with water and ethyl alcohol three times. The product was collected and grounded into powder by a mortar and pestle for further use.

SiO2@SiQDs composite was fabricated via a one-pot method by our previous work [18]. Briefly, 0.396 g silica nanospheres, 1.6 mL of ATPMS and a solution of SA (2 mL, 100 mM) were successively mixed by adding into a glass beaker containing 6.4 mL ultrapure water and stirred for 1 h. After being sonicated for 5 min, the resulting mixture was transferred into high-speed centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 10 min and washed three times with a mixture of water and anhydrous ethanol three times.

Before LFP imaging experiments, the collection of LFP samples should be conducted beforehand. Firstly, fingers of two donators (1 adult male and 1 adult female) were gently wiped over his or her nose and forehead. After that, LFPs were stamped onto the various substrates (glasses, transparent plastics, aluminum alloys, ceramic tiles, painted wood and paperboard). With the aid of squirrel brush, SiO2@SiQDs powders were deposited on the LFPs in few seconds. Finally, the excess powders were removed by gently flick the brush, and the images of developed LFPs were allowed to be photographed in day-light or by Video spectral comparator (VSC-8000/HS, Foster + freeman, UK) under 365 nm irradiation, respectively.

The SiQDs were mildly synthesized by using APTMS as silica source and SA as reduced agent at atmospheric temperature and normal pressure. As depicted in Fig. 1 a, the TEM image displayed a spherical and monodispersed morphology, and the size distribution fell in the range of 2 to 3 nm with an average grain size of 2.41 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) characterization confirmed the lattice spacing of 0.19 nm (inset of Fig. 1a), which matches well with the typical (220) of the cubic crystalline silicon. To study the optical property of our SiQDs, the absorption spectrum was firstly monitored. Obviously, a broad absorption band between 250 and 400 nm was observed with a tail in the near-visible region due to various π–π* (C = C) and n–π* (C = O) transitions. Furthermore, there were no sharp peaks in the near UV spectrum, which may be one of the significant differences in absorption character between silicon quantum dots and silicon nanoparticles [17,34]. The fluorescence excitation spectrum of SiQDs exhibited a strong green emission with the maximum emission wavelength at 515 nm under the optimal excitation wavelength around 380 nm (Fig. 1b), which was in line with the aforementioned absorption spectrum. The PLQY of as-prepared SiQDs was determined to be 19.8% using quinine sulfate as a reference (see more details in Supplementary data). As shown in Fig. 1 c, the fluorescence peak, which stayed approximately at 515 nm with the excitation wavelength ranging from 300 to 420 nm, performed an excitation-independent behavior, which indicated that our SiQDs were uniform in particle size. Additionally, the fluorescence lifetime was 6.97 ns and the luminescence decay curves were well-fitted with a biexponential equation (Fig. 1 d).

Before SiQDs can be used as a fluorescent probe, the demonstration of selectivity of SiQDs for Fe3+ sensing is necessary. Under 365 nm UV light, no significant fluorescence variation was visually observed when Zn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Na+, Ca2+, Al3+, Hg2+, Pb2+ and Cr3+ was added into SiQD solution, respectively. However, only the SiQDs mixed with Fe3+ presented an obvious decline in luminous brightness as shown in Fig. S1a. To further confirm this specific fluorescence quenching effect of SiQDs with Fe3+, the ratio of fluorescence intensity of different metal ions to blank (I/I0) was carried out (Fig. S1b). The results showed clearly that the fluorescence intensity of SiQDs added with Fe3+ was reduced by more than half, while after adding other ions in the same concentration, the solution remained most amounts of fluorescence intensity, indicating a high selectivity of our SiQDs for Fe3+ sensing.

To evaluate the high sensitivity of this SiQDs-based assay for Fe3+, a series of concentration-dependent trials were conducted in detail. As observed in Fig. 2 a and Fig. 2b, the fluorescence intensity decreased with the increase of Fe3+ concentration ranging from 0 to 1000 μM. The corresponding images of 365 nm UV radiation can be caught visually, as the rising of the Fe3+, the fluorescent color became gradually darker (Fig. 2c). A good linear relationship with R2 = 0.991 was gained between F/F0 and the concentration of Fe3+ in concentration range 2–1000 μM, and the detection limit was calculated to be 0.086 μM (3δ/s rule, whereδ corresponds to the standard deviation of 11 blank tests, and s denotes the standard curve slope) (Fig. 3d), where F0 and F means the fluorescence intensities of the SiQDs without and with Fe3+, respectively. Compared with other recently reported fluorescent Fe3+ probes (Table S1), this work performed best in LOD, suggesting a high sensitivity of our novel sensing method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application to the real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above conclusion, the sensitive detection of Fe3+ in real lake was made to investigate the utility value of our SiQD probe. To reveal the interfering ions in real lake sample, ICP-MS was aforehand performed (Table S2). As observed in Fig. S2a and Fig. S2b, the fluorescence intensity decreased with the increasing concentrations of Fe3+. Accordingly, the visual fluorescent color of probe can be caught, as the rising of the Fe3+, the fluorescent color became darker gradually (Fig. S2c). A good linear relationship with R2 = 0.993 was gained between F/F0 and the concentration of Fe3+ in concentration range 0.6–3.0 μM, and the detection limit was calculated to be 2.82 μM (Fig. S2d), which was far below the US and EU standard. Therefore, our SiQD probe proved a high sensitivity and stability to resist environmental interference for the direct analysis of Fe3+ in the real water, which can be used as a potentially applicable fluorescence probe.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism usually contains inner filter effect (IFE), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), dynamic quenching, and static quenching. However, both IFE and FRET cannot occur unless the absorption spectrum of quencher overlapped with the excitation or emission spectra of quantum dots [35]. To exclude the IFE and FRET mechanism in our system, optical properties of SiQDs and SiQDs-Fe3+ were compared. As showed in Fig. 3 a, there was no overlap between absorption spectrum of Fe3+ and excitation spectrum of SiQDs, and the absorbance of Fe3+ at 380 nm irradiation was very weak, indicating Fe3+ cannot block the excitation light or absorb the emission energy from SiQDs, which meant the impossibility of IFE in our system. Similarly, there was also no overlap between the UV absorption peak of Fe3+ and the emission spectrum of SiQDs, which suggested that the quenching process cannot be explained by FRET.

An effective way to explore the quenching mechanism is comparing the average fluorescence lifetime of SiQDs with SiQDs-Fe3+ complex. In theory, the fluorescence lifetime of fluorophore remains almost constant in static quenching process, whereas it is opposite in dynamic quenching [35]. As shown in Fig. 3b, the average fluorescence lifetime of SiQDs and SiQDs-Fe3+ were measured to be 6.972 ns and 7.03 ns, respectively. Therefore, it is reasonable to infer the static quenching mechanism between the interaction of Fe3+ and SiQDs.

To better explore the quenching mechanism, the quenching equation was calculated according to the Stern–Volmer equation [36,37]:
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
sv
Q
= 1 +
K
q τ 0
Q
where Kq is the association equilibrium constant; F0 is the fluorescence intensity without Fe3+; F is the fluorescence intensity after adding Fe3+.

As shown in Fig. S3a, a good linear Stern–Volmer relationship was observed in SiQDs-Fe3+ and Kq was calculated to be 1.05 × 1012 mol/s, which was much higher than 2.0 × 1010 mol/s as the threshold value [36]. Consequently, the result evidenced the presence of Fe3+ in the SiQD solution does not change the lifetime of SiQDs, and it can be concluded that Fe3+ quenched the fluorescence of SiQDs in a static mode.

Additionally, the high selectivity of SiQDs for Fe3+ sensing may be on account of the strong affinity interaction or faster chelating kinetics between Fe3+ ions and –OH, –NH2 of SiQDs, compared with other transition-metal ions [38]. The association constant of the SiQDs-Fe3+ complex could be calculated according to the Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal equation [39]:
1
F 0
- F
=
1
F 0
+
1
K
LB
F 0
Q
where KLB represents the association constant of SiQDs and Fe3+, F0 and F correspond to the fluorescence intensity of SiQDs at the emission of 515 nm without or with Fe3+, respectively; [Q] denotes the concentration of quencher (Fe3+).
As exhibited in Fig. S3b, the association constant of the SiQDs-Fe3+ complex was calculated to be 6.8 × 103 mol/L, which is basically in line with the results of similar works [40].

For a deeper understanding of the quenching effect of Fe3+ on SiQDs in molecular level, the comparison of FTIR spectra of SiQDs and SiQDs-Fe3+ complex was carried out. As described in Table S3 and Fig. S4, it was found that most of the characteristic peaks did not change significantly. However, the peaks at 3370 and 1600 cm−1 of SiQDs shifted to 3359 and 1594 cm−1 after the addition of Fe3+, which is attributed to the stretching and bending vibration of N–H, respectively [31]. In addition, it can be obviously observed that the intensity of the two peaks decreased greatly. Therefore, it is reasonable to speculate that there is an obvious interaction between Fe3+ and the amino group of SiQDs.

As a result, according to the UV–vis absorption spectrum analysis, FTIR spectrum analysis, fluorescence lifetime, association equilibrium constant and association constant calculation above, it can be concluded that our SiQDs can form a nonfluorescent and stable ground-state complex with quencher (Fe3+) as illustrated in Fig. 3c.

Since powder dusting method is principally a physical absorption, the high-resolution imaging of LFPs depends largely on the particle size and shape. In general, nanosized or spherical particles can adhere to fingerprint residues better than larger and irregular ones, resulting high developing sensitivity and selectivity. To characterize the morphological change of synthetic products before and after the formation of SiQDs, SEM and TEM detections were both performed. Fig. 4 a and 4b showed the monodispersed morphology of SiO2 and SiO2@SiQDs, separately. Unlike the original smooth surface of SiO2, the surface of SiO2@SiQDs was found full of bulges like the strawberry (Fig. 4b), indicating the successful location of SiQDs on silica nanospheres. Moreover, an evident reduction in average particle size from 147 nm of SiO2 to 130 nm of SiO2@SiQDs was monitored, which could be due to an etching effect under alkaline condition [18].

As is known, high fluorescence of developing powders is of great importance for LFP imaging because it means a high signal of ridge details. To that end, the fluorescence behavior of SiQDs powder obtained by natural drying from SiQD solution was firstly studied. The Fig. S5a manifested that the fluorescence emission intensity at 507 nm of SiQD powder was too weak to be clearly seen by the naked eyes (Fig. S5b), which should be ascribed to ACQ effect [29,30]. Therefore, to maintain the high SSF of SiQDs, the immobilization of these luminous quantum dots onto silica nanospheres to prevent them from aggregation is necessary. As shown in Fig. 5 a, a broad absorption band without obvious sharp peaks was presented in the ultraviolet region of SiO2@SiQDs powder, which matched well with its solution. More importantly, a surge in fluorescence emission intensity (inset of Fig. 5a) can be observed by 380 nm UV lamp compared with aforementioned SiQD powder, indicating the realization in self-quenching resistance of our composite powder. Furthermore, the excitation-independent behavior of our SiO2@SiQDs powder could be detected from 320 to 400 nm, which was also consistent with its PL property of SiQD solution. Notably, the PL spectrum of our composite powder suggested a maximum emission wavelength at 507 nm under 385 nm excitation (Fig. 5b), which had a certain blue shift compared with that of its solution. This blue shift of emission wavelength probably can be attributed to the quantum size effect [41]. In brief, the SiQDs on the surface of silica nanospheres may become smaller in particle size and irregular in shape because of the above etching effect, thus the emission wavelength wound be blue shifted, and the peak pattern also become less regular than that of SiQD solution. The fluorescence lifetime was 11.784 ns and well fitted with biexponential equation (Fig. 5c). Additionally, the fluorescence stability of this composite powder is one of the important factors to determine whether it can be applied and popularized. As depicted in Fig. S6a, our SiO2@SiQD powder can maintain 90% fluorescence intensity after 60 min of UV irradiation, which evidences its decent photobleaching resistance and potential to promote at crime scenes.

To compare the change of surface functional groups between SiO2 and SiO2@SiQDs, the measurement of FTIR analysis was done. Fig. 5d displayed the stretch vibration of Si-O bonding at 1095 cm−1 and bending vibration of H-O–H bonding at 1635 cm−1 both for two compounds. More importantly, the newly emerged peak at 1400 and 3226 cm−1 of SiO2@SiQDs, which was attributed to stretch vibration of C-N and N–H [42], respectively, suggested strongly the formation of amide bond. And beyond that, an obvious weakening of the bending vibration peak of Si-OH at 956 cm−1 of the composite powder indicated that a mass of silicon hydroxyl groups was replaced, serving an indirect evidence for the generation of amide bonds.

To further assure the existence of SiQDs on silica nanospheres, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was fulfilled. The full-scan X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy plot of SiO2@SiQDs manifested five main peaks, at 103.8, 153.8, 285.8, 400.8 and 532.8 eV, which was attributed to Si 2p, Si 2 s, C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s, respectively (Fig. S7). The high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectra confirmed the presence of C-C、C-N、C-O and C = O (Fig. S7b). The N 1 s spectra could be split into two peaks which corresponded to N-C, N–H, N-Si (Fig. S7c). The high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1 s revealed the O-Si, O-C (Fig. S7d). The Si 2p spectrum displayed three typical peaks could be ascribed to Si-C, Si-O (Fig. S7e). Given the above, the C-N, C = O, N–H, N-C Si-C and Si-O in the high-resolution XPS spectra can pose as further proof of the amide bonds as well as SiQDs on the surface of silica nanospheres.

As set forth, our SiO2@SiQDs nanospheres have nanosized dimension, round shape, nice monodispersity, as well as high fluorescence, and thus is expected to enable appropriate adsorption with fingerprint residues as high-resolution probes for LFP imaging. Briefly, the high resolution LFP imaging refers to the high developing contrast, high developing sensitivity and high developing sensitivity [38]. To verify the high developing contrast of SiO2@SiQDs as fluorescent probes for LFP imaging, fingerprints on glasses, transparent plastics, aluminum alloys, ceramic tiles, painted wood and paperboard were chosen as the substrates. As shown in Fig. 6 , these prints were well visualized not only on nonporous or smooth substrates (glasses, transparent plastics, aluminum alloys and ceramic tiles), but also on porous or rough surfaces (painted wood and paperboard). In dark condition, the UV induced green fluorescence could produce bright papillary ridges, indicating a high developing contrast of our SiO2@SiQDs.

The high developing sensitivity and selectivity signify not only high level of definition of ridges, but also low background contamination of developing powders. To verify the high developing sensitivity and selectivity, minutiae of a LFP on glass slide were analyzed in a higher magnification. As manifested in Fig. S8, most of the Level 2 characteristics, such as bifurcations, combinations, spurs, and islands can be legibly recognized. To check the developing performance of aged LFPs at a higher resolution, a commercial phosphor was selected as the control. The results reflected that a large number of sweat pores of a 15-d LFP could still be displayed with little background interference when the composite green powder was used (Fig. 7 a). By contrast, not merely the region of ridges, but also the furrows were contaminated with commercial phosphors, and no sweat pores can be visualized in the same condition (Fig. 7b), indicating a stronger hydrophobic interaction, derived from the nanosize, regular shape and monodispersity of SiO2@SiQDs, between the resultant powders and oily components in fingerprint residues [24,43]. The grey value analyses across the same zones (plotted by red lines) further indicated a much greater and stabler average contrast of peak-valley in Fig. 7a’ when SiO2@SiQD was applied, which conformed with the foregoing visual observation. In recent years, the quantitative evaluation of developing effect is becoming more necessary in fingerprint investigation [44]. Based on the analysis of gray images of two developed fingerprints in Fig. 7 with the aid of ImageJ software, the total developing selectivity of SiO2@SiQDs and commercial phosphors was calculated to be 2.32 and 1.01 (Fig. S9, S10), respectively, which is in line with the results of previous visual observation.

Consequently, our SiO2@SiQD composite powder was proved to be an ideal fluorescent probe for LFP imaging with higher developing sensitivity and selectivity than commercial phosphors, because the commercial ones are hardly optimized in the microscopic morphology of particles, which results in their poor adsorption with fingerprint residues [19].

In this work, we have designed a novel bifunctional fluorescent probe based on green emissive SiQDs with the quantum yield of 19.8% both for highly sensitive Fe3+ sensing and highly resolution LFP imaging. The mechanism investigation has manifested the ferric ion can statically quench the fluorescence of SiQDs in a linearity range of 2–1000 μM with the LOD of 0.086 μM in water. In addition, this fluorescence sensor for Fe3+ detection was proved effective in a real water sample. To conquer the ACQ effect, we constructed a SiO2@SiQD composite powder, which maintained the green fluorescence of primitive SiQDs. The amide bond on the surface of silica nanospheres was inferred to anchor and disperse these SiQDs away from self-aggregation. Based on such an ingenious design, the SiO2@SiQDs were demonstrated to be an ideal probe for LFP imaging with high developing sensitivity, high developing selectivity, and high developing contrast. In summary, we have not only developed a bifunctional fluorescent probe for metal ion sensing and forensic science but also proposed a novel methodology for the realization of high SSF of SiQDs.
Di Peng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Zihe Zhao: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Visualization, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Chongqing Municipal Bureau of Science and Technology (cstc2021jcyj-msxmX0713, CSTB2022TIAD-KPX0109), Chongqing Education Commission (KJZD-M202000301, KJZD-K202200302), Innovation Research Group Project of Universities in Chongqing (CXQT21033), and Southwest University of Political Science and Law (2021XZNDJDYB-07, 2021XZXS-137).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122827.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
